metadata.subject: Physical and Health Education
metadata.description: Physical and Health Education 8 develops students' physical literacy, health knowledge, and social-emotional skills. Students refine fundamental movement skills and apply them across a wide range of physical activities, learn to monitor and improve their own fitness, and develop knowledge and skills for healthy decision-making across physical, emotional, mental, and social dimensions of health. First Peoples perspectives on holistic well-being and community health are integrated throughout.
metadata.general_description: A comprehensive course developing physical literacy, fitness knowledge, healthy active living habits, social-emotional skills, and health decision-making — building the foundation for lifelong physical activity participation and personal well-being.

big_ideas.statement: Daily participation in different types of physical activity influences our physical literacy and personal health and fitness goals.
big_ideas.elaboration: Engaging regularly in a variety of physical activities — rather than specializing early — develops broad physical literacy and helps individuals discover activities they will enjoy throughout their lives.

big_ideas.statement: Lifelong participation in physical activity has many benefits and is an essential part of a healthy lifestyle.
big_ideas.elaboration: Physical activity contributes to physical health, mental well-being, social connection, and quality of life across the lifespan. Developing positive habits and competence in movement in adolescence supports lifelong active living.

big_ideas.statement: Healthy choices influence our physical, emotional, and mental well-being.
big_ideas.elaboration: Decisions about nutrition, sleep, physical activity, substance use, and relationships collectively shape a person's well-being. Developing the knowledge, skills, and self-awareness to make healthy choices is a core life competency.

big_ideas.statement: Healthy relationships can help us lead rewarding and fulfilling lives.
big_ideas.elaboration: The quality of our relationships — with friends, family, partners, and community — significantly affects our health and happiness. Developing communication, respect, and boundary-setting skills supports healthy relational life.

big_ideas.statement: Advocating for the health and well-being of others connects us to our community.
big_ideas.elaboration: Health is not only individual — it is shaped by the communities we live in. Developing the skills and motivation to advocate for health equity, inclusion, and community well-being connects personal health to social responsibility.

competencies.raw: Develop, refine, and apply fundamental movement skills in various physical activities; develop and apply movement concepts and strategies in different activities; apply methods for monitoring and adjusting exertion levels; demonstrate safety, fair play, and leadership in physical activities; identify preferred types of physical activity.
i_can_statements: I can develop, refine, and apply fundamental movement skills — locomotor, non-locomotor, and manipulative — across a range of physical activities.
I can apply movement concepts (body, space, effort, and relationships) and strategies to improve my performance.
I can monitor my exertion levels using heart rate, RPE scale, or other methods and adjust my effort appropriately.
I can demonstrate safety, fair play, and leadership in physical activities.
I can identify the types of physical activity I most enjoy and explain why.

competencies.raw: Participate daily in fitness-enhancing physical activity; describe how school, home, and community activities influence health; develop strategies for promoting healthy eating choices; assess factors influencing healthy choices; identify and analyze health message sources and their behavioural influence; identify and apply personal healthy-living goal strategies; reflect on outcomes and assess personal goal strategies.
i_can_statements: I can participate daily in physical activity designed to enhance and maintain my fitness.
I can describe how activities at school, home, and in my community influence my overall health.
I can develop and apply strategies for making healthy eating choices.
I can assess the factors — social, cultural, economic — that influence my health choices.
I can identify and critically analyze the sources of health messages I receive.
I can set personal healthy-living goals, apply strategies to work toward them, and reflect on my progress.

competencies.raw: Propose strategies for avoiding and/or responding to unsafe or exploitative situations; propose strategies for responding to discrimination, stereotyping, and bullying; propose strategies for developing and maintaining healthy relationships; create strategies for promoting school and community well-being.
i_can_statements: I can propose effective strategies for avoiding and responding to unsafe, abusive, or exploitative situations.
I can propose strategies for responding to — and standing up against — discrimination, stereotyping, and bullying.
I can propose and apply strategies for developing and maintaining healthy, respectful relationships.
I can create and implement strategies to promote the health and well-being of my school and community.

competencies.raw: Describe and assess strategies for promoting mental well-being; describe and assess strategies for managing mental health and substance use problems; create and assess strategies for managing physical, emotional, and social changes during puberty; explore and describe transition and change impacts on identities.
i_can_statements: I can describe and assess strategies — including physical activity — for promoting my own and others' mental well-being.
I can describe the signs of mental health challenges and assess strategies for getting help and supporting others.
I can create and apply strategies for managing the physical, emotional, and social changes of adolescence.
I can explore and describe how transitions and changes in life — puberty, school, family — affect identity and well-being.

areas.name: Fundamental Movement Skills and Physical Activity
areas.raw: Fundamental movement skills, movement concepts, physical activity types.
topics: Non-locomotor skills: balancing, bending, twisting, lifting, and stretching
Locomotor skills: running, jumping, hopping, skipping, galloping, and rolling
Manipulative skills: throwing, catching, kicking, striking, bouncing, and dribbling
Movement concepts: body awareness, spatial awareness, effort awareness, and relationships
Physical activity types: individual/dual activities, rhythmic activities (dance, gymnastics), games with rules
Movement strategies: teamwork, game play, positioning, and decision-making in activity

areas.name: Fitness and Training
areas.raw: FITT, SAID principles, fitness monitoring, and effects of physical activity.
topics: Components of fitness: cardiovascular endurance, muscular strength and endurance, flexibility, body composition
FITT principle: Frequency, Intensity, Type, and Time — applying to personal fitness planning
SAID principle: Specific Adaptation to Imposed Demand — training specificity
Methods of monitoring exertion: heart rate, Rate of Perceived Exertion (RPE), talk test
Effects of physical activity: muscle and bone strengthening, cardiovascular health, stress and anxiety reduction
Goal-setting for fitness: applying SMART goals to personal fitness improvement

areas.name: Healthy Living and Decision Making
areas.raw: Nutrition, sleep, sexual health, substance use, safety, and health information.
topics: Nutrition: balanced eating, reading food labels, influences on food choices, and healthy meal planning
Sleep: importance of sleep for health, development, and academic performance
Sexual health: puberty and adolescent development, sexual decision-making, consent, and STI basics
Substance use: alcohol, tobacco, cannabis — short and long-term effects, addiction, and refusal skills
Health information sources: evaluating credibility of websites, social media, advertising, and professionals
Emergency response: basic first aid principles, when and how to get help
Online safety: digital citizenship, privacy, recognizing predatory tactics, and seeking help

areas.name: Social and Community Health
areas.raw: Bullying, discrimination, relationships, and community well-being.
topics: Bullying and cyberbullying: forms, consequences, bystander responsibility, and reporting
Discrimination and stereotyping: recognizing and responding to bias in school and community
Healthy relationships: characteristics, communication, respect, boundaries, and consent
Unhealthy and abusive relationships: recognizing warning signs and seeking help
Community well-being: advocacy, peer support, and inclusive practices
First Peoples perspectives on holistic health and community care

areas.name: Mental Well-being
areas.raw: Mental health, stress, puberty, identity, and resilience.
topics: Mental health: understanding the spectrum from well-being to mental illness
Stress and anxiety: causes, effects on the body, and coping strategies
Depression: signs, symptoms, how to support yourself and others
Physical and emotional changes of puberty: what to expect and how to navigate
Identity: exploring how gender, culture, family, peers, and experiences shape who we are
Resilience: developing coping strategies, emotional regulation, and self-compassion
